next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 17

Paul watched Tommy through the sliding glass door for a long time before he went out.

Tommy was in the Adirondack chair closest to the railing, Martin's chair whether anybody called it that or not, with a beer balanced on the armrest he wasn't touching. The propane heater was off. The temperature had dropped into the low fifties after sunset, and Tommy was sitting there in a T-shirt and jeans like cold had quit mattering. His hands were on his knees. He was looking at the lake.

Joel was in the study. He'd been there since they read the fire document, going through Martin's careful handwriting again, making notes on his legal pad, building the case against a man he'd known for twenty-five years with the same flat precision he used for people he'd never met. Joel had wanted to go straight to Mallick.

"We hand her this tonight," Joel had said. "The document, the photos on Cliff's phone, the timeline. She's got it."

"I know."

"Then let's go."

"Give me an hour."

Joel looked at him the way he looked at clients who were about to make their own worst decision. "Why?"

Paul didn't have a clean answer. Martin was dead. Martin had spent twenty-two years protecting Tommy. Paul needed to look at Tommy's face when the protection ran out.

"Because I need to."

Joel didn't push it. The job had taught him what arguing couldn't move. "I'll be in the study. If he..."

"He won't."

"You don't know that, Paul."

"I know Tommy."
open_hooks_to_carry: Joel and Paul on the deck comparing notes — Paul's suspicion about Martin-Tommy, Joel's observations about Tommy-Cliff
They go to the study and find the fire document in the safe — the envelope has been unfolded twice by different hands
Joel reads Martin's account of the 2002 fire and Ray Toomey's death
The picture assembles: Cliff found the document, Cliff leveraged Tommy, Tommy killed Cliff
Joel and Paul must decide: go to Mallick, confront Tommy, or protect the group one last time the way Martin would have
Joel's legal mind and Paul's loyalty to Martin's memory should pull in opposite directions

payoffs_to_land.description: Joel and Paul find and read the fire document that Cliff discovered in Ch 8 and used as leverage in Ch 12. The crease pattern reveals it was handled by multiple people. The full picture of the murder motive is now assembled.

prev_tail_excerpt:
She tapped it.

At first it was exactly what he'd promised: beer bottles on wood, somebody arguing about waiver priority, Nate laughing too hard at his own line, someone calling someone else an idiot with real affection under it. The group before the second death. The group when it could still pass for itself.

Then plates started moving. Chairs scraped back. Voices spread out, farther from the phone.

Cliff's voice came in close and low, not meant for the whole table but caught anyway.

"Ten-thirty. Dock. You and me."

A pause. Three seconds of silverware and chair legs.

Tommy answered from farther off, rough enough that she would have known him without the weekend of interviews.

"Not in here."

Cliff again, just as low. "Ten-thirty."

Another pause. Then Tommy, flat and final.

"Fine."

Mallick hit pause.

Not a confession. More than a hunch. Tommy had lied about the disagreement. The rest of his timeline had just gotten a lot harder to believe. Cliff had expected him at the dock.

She picked up her pen and wrote one more line under Renaldi's name.

*Audio: Haywood and Renaldi set dock meeting for 10:30 PM during dinner file.*
secondary_character_beats: Joel's public defender instincts should make him aware of what this evidence means legally — and what it doesn't prove without confession
state_deltas_required: Joel and Paul assembling the full picture from civilian evidence
Joel and Paul know Tommy killed Cliff — the question is what they do with it
